title: After three heatwaves, French people worry: 'Next year will be worse. How will we cope?'

Wildfire haze hangs over eastern US ahead of World Cup final
subhed: The wildfires burning in Canada have sent noxious smog to settle over much of the eastern United States. At one point on Saturday, the smoke made New York the  most polluted city in the world – only one day before the World Cup final is to be held across the river in New Jersey in an open-air stadium.

title: War is undermining social contract between Putin and Russians
subhed: The spread of Ukrainian attacks across the country is deepening Russians' concerns about the regime's ability to protect their basic needs, as the economic situation deteriorates.

title: 'We are trapped in a climate dystopia, a black comedy scenario, where the grotesque rivals the Orwellian farce'
subhed: As heatwaves multiply, public debate often amounts to little more than empty gestures. Recent government decisions to cut green funds and return energy-inefficient homes to the rental market reveal the denial prevailing among much of the political class, writes Le Monde columnist Stéphane Foucart.

title: How Dakar caught surfing's new wave: 'It has become a path to success and a way of life'
subhed: The Senegalese coast, with its small villages and beaches where Atlantic swells create powerful waves that draw surfers from around the world, has produced a new generation of young champions eager to ride. Every day, they take on the ocean at about 12 spots along this wild, little-known shoreline.

title: Behind the scenes of Macron's last G7, as told by photographer Kamil Zihnioglu
subhed: There is protocol, and then there is Donald Trump. That, in essence, is what photographer Kamil Zihnioglu took away from his three days covering the G7 summit in Evian-les-Bains for Le Monde.

title: How the 2016 Nice terrorist attack unfolded, minute-by-minute
subhed: On July 14, 2016, a terrorist drove a truck into crowds gathered on the famed Promenade des Anglais in the southern French city, killing 86 people. Ten years later, Le Monde looks back at how the tragedy unfolded.
